coredata.dc:title: Portable smartphone-assisted RGB-dependent ratiometric sensing platform for the detection of tetrachloro-p-benzoquinone in river samples
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Meng
Liu, Ting
Yin, Chenhui
Jiang, Xinxin
Sun, Qijun
Gao, Lei
Niu, Na
Chen, Ligang
Gang, Huixin
coredata.dc:description: Tetrachloro-p-benzoquinone (TC-PBQ) can be harmful to the environment as a pesticide residue, but its analytical detection has been largely neglected. In this work, a smartphone-assisted RGB-dependent TC-PBQ ratiometric sensing platform for rapid recognition is introduced. Here, the red-emitting mercaptopropionic acid-capped CdTe quantum dots (MPA-capped CdTe) as the reaction unit can be quenched by TC-PBQ, while the blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) as the reference signal remain unchanged. The limit of detection (LOD) of the sensing platform can reach 59 nM in aqueous solution. By constructing an analysis model with the system RBG value, the smartphone-assisted sensor-coated fluorescent test paper can complete the qualitative and quantitative visual detection of TC-PBQ (LOD is 93 nM). The applicability of smartphone-dependent RGB analysis significantly simplifies and speeds up the operation process, which provides a new analytical method for real-time analysis of TC-PBQ in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
RGB analysis
Smartphone identification
Environmental pollutants
Tetrachloro-p-benzoquinone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TC-PBQ by fluorescence spectrometer
Fluorescent test strip smartphone platform visually detect TC-PBQ
Detection of TC-PBQ in real sample river water
Study on the fluorescent properties and quenching mechanism of probe
Response of TC-PBQ to CDs/ MPA-capped CdTe
Visual detection of TC-PBQ by smartphone sensing platform
Analysis of TC-PBQ in river water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and application of ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for dual-mode TC-PBQ sensing.

TEM spectra of CDs (A) and MPA-capped CdTe (B), insets are the corresponding lattice fringe spacing. (C) XRD spectrum. (D) infrared spectrum. (E) XPS full scan spectrum. (F)-(H) High resolution of CDs. (I) High resolution of MPA-capped CdTe.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the corresponding substances, insets are fluorescence images of CDs (a), MPA-capped CdTe (b) and CDs/MPA-capped CdTe (c) under UV light. (B) UV spectra of TC-PBQ, CDs/MPA-capped CdTe and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/MPA-capped CdTe. Fluorescence lifetimes of (C) CDs of CDs/MPA-capped CdTe and (D) MPA-capped CdTe of CDs/MPA-capped CdTe before and after the addition of TC-PBQ.

Adjust the fluorescence intensity ratio of CDs and MPA-capped CdTe to 1:1 (A), 1:2 (B) and 1:3 (C). (D) Linear plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I460/I690) and different concentrations of TC-PBQ. (E) CIE chromaticity diagram. (F) Selectivity.

(A) The internal structure of the smartphone sensing assistance platform. Linear relationship between B/R and TC-PBQ concentration of probe solution (B) and fluorescent test paper (C), and the corresponding images under 365 nm UV lamp.

head.ce:title: Portable smartphone-assisted RGB-dependent ratiometric sensing platform for the detection of tetrachloro-p-benzoquinone in river samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization
Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Heilongjiang River Fisheries Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Fishery Sciences, Harbin 150010, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Heilongjiang River Fisheries Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Fishery Sciences, Harbin 150010, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Horticulture & Landscape Architecture, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Horticulture & Landscape Architecture, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparation and application of ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for dual-mode TC-PBQ sensing.

Tetrachloro-p-benzoquinone (TC-PBQ) can be harmful to the environment as a pesticide residue, but its analytical detection has been largely neglected. In this work, a smartphone-assisted RGB-dependent TC-PBQ ratiometric sensing platform for rapid recognition is introduced. Here, the red-emitting mercaptopropionic acid-capped CdTe quantum dots (MPA-capped CdTe) as the reaction unit can be quenched by TC-PBQ, while the blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) as the reference signal remain unchanged. The limit of detection (LOD) of the sensing platform can reach 59 nM in aqueous solution. By constructing an analysis model with the system RBG value, the smartphone-assisted sensor-coated fluorescent test paper can complete the qualitative and quantitative visual detection of TC-PBQ (LOD is 93 nM). The applicability of smartphone-dependent RGB analysis significantly simplifies and speeds up the operation process, which provides a new analytical method for real-time analysis of TC-PBQ in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the commonly used pesticides, tetrachloro-p-benzoquinone (TC-PBQ) is often emerged as an agricultural seed dressing to effectively prevent pests and diseases in crops to maintain normal growth [1,2]. As human activities increase and ecosystems cycle, levels of TC-PBQ in environmental water samples are accumulating year by year. This benzoquinone is an irritant to the mucous membranes, respiratory tract and skin of living organisms and could have adverse effects. In particular, the strongly electrophilic TC-PBQ forms covalent adducts with human DNA, which can cause damage to DNA [3]. However, the detection of TC-PBQ in environmental samples is largely ignored. Generally, the detection of TC-PBQ can be analyzed by chromatography and mass spectrometry [4,5]. However, these analytical techniques require expensive equipment and complex sample pre-treatment limiting their application in on-site analysis. The fluorescence analytical technique with simple, operable and sensitive features can be considered as a new analytical method for TC-PBQ sensing.

Although there is a quantity of fluorescence analytical methods available for the detection of contaminants in water, most of these fluorescent sensing probes are currently based on a single fluorescence 'off ' or 'on' strategy [6–8]. These probes, which use a single response emission signal, are susceptible to interference from a variety of experimental factors, and the color change is only an increase or decrease in brightness, making visual detection difficult. In contrast, the ratiometric sensing platform combines an internal reference probe with different emission and a signal response probe, providing a built-in correction function through two emission intensity ratios, reducing the influence of external environmental and other interfering factors and making the results more accurate [9–11]. In addition, dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probes provide richer color changes, allowing the probe to exhibit a wide range of color variations in response to the object to be measured, making visualization more obvious and more conducive to rapid detection [12,13].

Paper sensors are gaining popularity among researchers due to the portability, affordability, and transportability of paper substrates [14–16]. Sensor-coated fluorescent test strips may bring the advantages of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probes to on-site rapid detection applications. In addition, with the development of technology, the functions of portable smartphones are becoming more and more abundant, which makes it rise rapidly in the field of analytical chemistry [17–19]. In particular, the RGB value of the target image can be identified by the application software of the smartphone after being captured by the camera, and then an analysis model is established in combination with the concentration of the analyte. Inspired by this, we envisioned whether a dual-emission TC-PBQ sensor-coated fluorescent test paper could be combined with a portable smartphone-based RGB-dependent sensing analysis model to enable rapid visual analysis of TC-PBQ.

In this work, a smartphone-assisted RGB-dependent dual-emission ratiometric sensing platform was fabricated for rapid visual analysis of TC-PBQ in aqueous environments (Fig. 1 ). The sensing platform (CDs/MPA-capped CdTe) is simply obtained by physically mixing mercaptopropionic acid-capped CdTe quantum dots (MPA-capped CdTe) with CDs. When used for fluorescence sensing analysis, the blue fluorescence of CDs in the system remains unchanged, while the red fluorescence of MPA-capped CdTe gradually weakens through the dynamic quenching mechanism of TC-PBQ. On this basis, the prepared sensor-coated fluorescent test paper showed a color change from peach red to pink, lilac purple, and finally bright blue with the increase of TC-PBQ concentration. Combined with the smartphone-assisted RGB-dependent sensing analysis model, the identification of TC-PBQ is more accurate. Finally, a strategy based on reading out the analytical signal from the application has achieved rapid detection of TC-PBQ in real river water.

Proline (1.0 g) dissolved in distilled water (10 mL) was placed in a stainless-steel autoclave and reacted for 6 h at 200 °C in a constant temperature blast drying oven. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the CDs solution was obtained by filtration using a disposable syringe and a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The final CDs were stored in a 4 °C freezer for later use.

The synthesis of red fluorescent MPA-capped CdTe were referred to our previous work [12,20]. The molar ratio of Cd2+: sodium hexametaphosphate: MPA: TeO3 2- is 1: 2.1: 2.5: 0.15. Briefly, CdCl2 2·.5H2O (0.4 mmol), sodium hexametaphosphate (0.84 mmol), and MPA (1 mmol) were added sequentially to deionized water (100 mL) with stirring. The pH of the solution was then adjusted to 8.0 by 1 mol L-1 NaOH. After stirring for 1 min, Na2TeO3 (0.06 mmol) was added to the system to react for 10 min, and then the mixed solution was concentrated and refluxed at 100 °C. After 10 min of reaction, N2H4·H2O (4.8 mL) was added to the above solution, and the reaction was continued for 3 h. Finally, the solution was centrifuged by a high-speed centrifuge to obtain MPA-capped CdTe solids. The solid powder obtained was redissolved in deionized water after drying in an oven for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC-PBQ by fluorescence spectrometer
ce:section.ce:para: CDs solution (100 μL), MPA-capped CdTe solution (300 μL) and BR buffer (600 μL) were sequentially added to the centrifuge tube. Subsequently, a series of concentrations of TC-PBQ standard solutions (0–100 μM) were also added to the above system. Fluorescence detection was performed after sufficient reaction at room temperature. The excitation wavelength of the fluorescence spectrofluorimeter was set to 320 nm, and the fluorescence changes were observed in the wavelength range of 400–800 nm. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I460/I690) between CDs (I460) and MPA-capped CdTe (I690) was calculated for the concentration analysis of TC-PBQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent test strip smartphone platform visually detect TC-PBQ
ce:section.ce:para: The non-fluorescent filter paper (Whatman NO.1) was cut into circular discs (d = 1 cm) and then soaked in the prepared CDs/MPA-capped CdTe solution. After soaking for 30 min, the test paper was dried naturally at room temperature in the dark to obtain the sensor-coated fluorescent test paper. TC-PBQ solutions of different concentrations were added dropwise to the prepared test paper. The system after reaction with TC-PBQ, including the solution and test paper, was then photographed with a smartphone. The RGB value of the picture is obtained by identifying the color recognition extractor of the WeChat applet. Finally, an analysis model of TC-PBQ concentration and RGB value was established to realize the rapid visual detection of TC-PBQ in the field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC-PBQ in real sample river water
ce:section.ce:para: The real samples in the experiment were taken from the river water in Harbin, Chain. The real sample river water was filtered through an aqueous filter (0.22 μm), and spiked samples were obtained by adding a series of concentrations of TC-PBQ solutions. The detection procedure is as described in 2.3 and 2.4.

Firstly, the structural characteristics of the prepared two materials were characterized. The microstructure of the material was observed by TEM images. The CDs synthesized from proline in Fig. 2
A are uniformly distributed with an average size of approximately 4–5 nm. And it can be seen from its inset that the CDs with clear lattice fringes have a fringe spacing of 0.18 nm. As shown in Fig. 2 B, the MPA-capped CdTe exhibit spherical morphology with a diameter of about 0.3 nm. Similarly, its lattice fringe spacing of about 0.30 nm can be observed in the inset of Fig. 2 B. Fig. 2 C shows the XRD spectrum of the as-prepared material. The broad peak at 2θ = 21.2° indicates the amorphous nature of CDs. The diffraction peaks located at (111), (220) and (311) respectively implied that the MPA-capped CdTe have a face-centered cubic structure [21]. The functional group structures of related substances were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 D). The spectra of CDs can be decomposed into O-H stretching vibrations (3402 cm−1), C-H stretching vibrations and bending vibrations (1490 cm−1 and 2968 cm−1), C = O stretching vibrations (1599 cm−1) and stretching C-N (1337 cm−1) vibration [22]. The surface functional groups of MPA-capped CdTe can be resolved as –OH (3428 cm−1), C = O (1204 cm−1) and –COO (1409 cm−1 and 1621 cm−1) vibrations. In addition, the characteristic absorption peak of -SH (2580 cm−1) attributed to MPA is not shown in the spectrum, which indicates that the thiol group has been covalently converted to a Cd-S coordination bond. This confirms that MPA has successfully modified CdTe quantum dots [23].

The samples were elementally analyzed by XPS characterization (Fig. 2 E). The full scan spectrum of CD exhibits three characteristic peaks, C1s (284.9 eV), N1s (400.52 eV) and O1s (500.3 eV). In the high-resolution spectrum, the C1s fit peaks were resolved as C-C, C-O, C-N, and C = O bonds (Fig. 2 F). Similarly, the fitted peaks in N1s correspond to N-H and C-N bonds, respectively (Fig. 2 G). Two fitted peaks for O1s indicate the presence of C-OH and C = O groups (Fig. 2 H). This indicates that the surface of the synthesized CDs is rich in amino and hydroxyl groups [24]. Likewise, the presence of characteristic peaks of Cd3d and Te3d can be seen in the XPS spectra of MPA-capped CdTe. The presence of O1s peaks in the spectra may be due to the oxidation of the quantum dots surface. Among them, the XPS spectra of Cd element and metallic Te can be resolved into Cd3d3/2, Cd3d5/2 and Te3d3/2, Te3d5/2, respectively. (Fig. 2 I). It is further indicated that Cd and Te elements exist in the as-prepared MPA-capped CdTe. The results of the above structural characterization prove the successful synthesis of the two materials, which can be used for the preparation of subsequent dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the fluorescent properties and quenching mechanism of probe
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, MPA-capped CdTe, and the CDs/MPA-capped CdTe fluorescent probe are shown in Fig. 3
A. The CDs/MPA-capped CdTe shows two emission peaks at 460 nm and 690 nm, which correspond to the emission peaks of CDs and MPA-capped CdTe alone, respectively. CDs and MPA-capped CdTe emit strong blue and red fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, respectively (insets a and b of Fig. 3 A). Unlike the individual luminophores, the ratiometric fluorescent probe obtained by simple physical mixing exhibits purple-red fluorescence (inset c of Fig. 3 A). To illustrate the fluorescence stability of the prepared probes, the time-based fluorescence intensity changes were tested. From the results in Fig. S1, it can be concluded that the fluorescence intensity ratio (I690/I460) of MPA-capped CdTe to CDs remained almost unchanged within 1 h, proving the excellent fluorescence stability of the constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe CDs/MPA-capped CdTe. After the introduction of TC-PBQ, it can be seen that the peak at 460 nm assigned to CDs remains unchanged, while the peak at 690 nm of MPA-capped CdTe is significantly quenched. This means that the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe with CDs as the reference unit and MPA-capped CdTe as the reaction unit can be used as an analytical sensor for TC-PBQ.

To explore the quenching mechanism of the ratiometric fluorescent probe CDs/MPA-capped CdTe, we first investigated the relationship between the UV absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of related substances (Fig. 3 B). As can be clearly observed in the spectrum, the absorption peak of TC-PBQ does not overlap significantly with the two emission peaks of CDs/MPA-capped CdTe. This means that energy transfer is not the main reason for the fluorescence quenching of CDs/MPA-CdTe. The possibility of Förster resonance energy transfer and internal filtering effects can be ruled out [25,26]. Because both quenching mechanisms require the analyte and the fluorescent material to have obvious spectral overlap phenomenon [27]. The quenching mechanism of TC-PBQ on CDs/MPA-CdTe is mainly due to photo-induced electron transfer. As a classical electron acceptor, TC-PBQ can shuttle electrons from the conduction band to the valence band of excited luminescent materials, resulting in the quenching of the emission intensity of CDs/MPA-CdTe [28]. Next, the fluorescence lifetime test was carried out on the ratiometric fluorescent probe CDs/MPA-capped CdTe. The results showed that the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs/MPA-capped CdTe (CDs) in the presence and absence of TC-PBQ were 4.80 ns and 4.82 ns, respectively (Fig. 3 C). This indicated that TC-PBQ had no effect on the fluorescence lifetime of CDs. The fluorescence lifetime of the ratiometric fluorescent probe CDs/MPA-capped CdTe (MPA-capped CdTe) was 62.3 ns without TC-PBQ. After the addition of TC-PBQ, the MPA-capped CdTe in the ratiometric fluorescent probe combined with TC-PBQ and interacted with each other, resulting in a significant decrease in the fluorescence lifetime. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs/MPA-capped CdTe (MPA-capped CdTe) changed to 43.6 ns (Fig. 3 D). This shows that the addition of the quencher will significantly change the fluorescence lifetime of the ratiometric fluorescent probe CDs/MPA-capped CdTe, and it can be concluded that the dynamic quenching mechanism is also involved in the fluorescence quenching [29,30]. In summary, the response and quenching mechanisms between TC-PBQ and CDs/MPA-CdTe are photoinduced electron transfer and dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of TC-PBQ to CDs/ MPA-capped CdTe
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to present a series of continuous and wide-ranging color changes during the detection process for the most recognizable visualization. The fluorescence intensity ratios of CDs (460 nm) and MPA-capped CdTe (690 nm) were explored, and then the fluorescence color change of the probe was observed after introducing a series of concentration gradients of TC-PBQ solution (0–8 μM). Apparently, with the increase of TC-PBQ concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 460 nm remained almost constant, while the fluorescence intensity at 690 nm gradually decreased. When the I460/I690 ratio was 1:1, the color of the fluorescence changed from lilac purple to bright blue (Fig. 4
A). When the ratio was 1:2, the fluorescent probes were observed to exhibit a wide range of color changes from peach red to lilac purple and finally bright blue (Fig. 4 B). When the fluorescence intensity ratio of the two reached 1:3, the color of the fluorescent probe can only change from peach red to lilac purple (Fig. 4 C). And due to the increase of MPA-capped CdTe content, the probe with an intensity ratio of 1:3 also requires a larger concentration of the quencher (TC-PBQ), otherwise the fluorescence of red MPA-capped CdTe cannot be quenched to the minimum. Therefore, we fixed the fluorescence intensity ratio of blue CDs and red MPA-capped CdTe as an optimal ratio of 1:2. In order to obtain stable detection results, the effect of reaction time on the fluorescence quenching of the system was investigated. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity ratio I460/I690 remained stable after 4 min, so it was designated as the reaction time of the system. In addition, the effect of the pH value of the reaction system on the detection of TC-PBQ was discussed. As shown in Fig. S3, when the pH＜7, the I460/I690 values gradually increased before and after the addition of TC-PBQ. This may be due to the deprotonation of –NH2 and –OH on the surface of CDs to protect the structure of CDs from being destroyed and the fluorescence intensity to remain stable, while the irreversible oxidation of the surface of MPA-capped CdTe reduces the fluorescence intensity. When the pH＞7, the I460/I690 values of the two systems gradually decreased. Under strong alkaline conditions, Cd2+ will combine with OH– to form hydroxide precipitation, which eventually leads to a drop in fluorescence intensity [31]. Therefore, pH = 7 was selected for the best detection of TC-PBQ.

In addition, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I460/I690 exhibited good linearity with TC-PBQ in the concentration range of 0.2–8 μM, and the linear equation is y = 0.5194 + 0.1302x (where × is the concentration of TC-PBQ, y is the fluorescence intensity ratio of blue CDs and red MPA-capped CdTe, Fig. 4 D). The correlation coefficient of the linear equation was R2 = 0.9987. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 59 nM as the ratio of 3σ to K, where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 11) and K is the slope of the calibration curve [32,33]. Fig. 4 E shows the CIE coordinates of the fluorescence emission color of the CDs/MPA-capped CdTe sensor corresponding to the concentration of TC-PBQ, which indicates that the sensing process exhibits a wide range of CIE chromaticity changes. To evaluate the excellent specificity of the prepared fluorescent probes for TC-PBQ, selectivity and interference experiments were performed. Pb2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, NO3 –, F-, Cl-, chlorpyrifos (CP), dimethoate (DM), carbendazim (CB), carbendazim (MBC) was selected as the interfering substance and introduced to the fluorescent system at a concentration of 10 μM, respectively. The results are illustrated in Fig. 4 F, and these interferents have little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric fluorescence system. This is because the surface of prepared CDs/MPA-capped CdTe is negatively charged, and positively charged TC-PBQ can be adsorbed on its surface through electrostatic interaction. And the unique benzoquinone structure of TC-PBQ is a typical electron acceptor, which can shuttle electrons from the conduction band to the valence band of excited CDs/MPA-terminated CdTe [34]. This will cause the fluorescence emission of CD/MPA-terminated CdTe to be quenched. Other pesticides were less responsive. Moreover, the anti-interference ability of the system is also expressed by the fluorescence curve. As indicated in in the inset of Fig. 4 F, curve a is the fluorescence intensity of the probe when no substance is added. When these interfering substances (10 μM) were added simultaneously, the fluorescence at 690 nm in the ratiometric fluorescent probe was only quenched by about 12% (curve b). When 6 μM TC-PBQ solution was added, the fluorescence intensity decreased by about 82% (curve c). This proves that the prepared CDs/MPA-capped CdTe ratiometric fluorescent probe has good anti-interference performance against other common substances and can be used for highly selective detection of TC-PBQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of TC-PBQ by smartphone sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 5
A shows the internal structure of the smartphone sensing assistance platform, which includes a camera obscura, UV light and a camera hole for the phone. Next, the TC-PBQ reaction system was analyzed using the camera function of the smartphone and the RGB-dependent analysis sensing model of the application software.[35]. As shown in Fig. 5 B, with the introduction of TC-PBQ, the color of the CDs/MPA-capped CdTe solution gradually transitioned from red to light purple at the beginning, and finally changed to blue. After using a smartphone camera to obtain a series of photos of the solution color, use the WeChat applet color recognition software to identify and obtain the corresponding RBG value. It can be plainly seen from the figure that the B/R value has a close relationship with the concentration of TC-PBQ, showing a good linear relationship in the range of TC-PBQ concentration of 0.25–8 μM, and the correlation coefficient is R2 = 0.9985. The LOD was calculated to be 80 nM. As shown in Fig. 5 C, the color of the fluorescent test paper coated with the CDs/MPA-capped CdTe sensing platform was rose red, and after adding different concentrations of TC-PBQ to the test paper, the color changed from peach red to pink, lilac purple, and bright blue finally. An analytical model established by identifying the RGB values of the reaction test paper found a good linear relationship between B/R and TC-PBQ concentration [36]. And the linear equation is y = 0.4130x + 0.8311 (R2 = 0.9928), and the calculated detection limit is 93 nM. Therefore, the fluorescent test paper coated with CDs/MPA-capped CdTe sensing platform combined with the portable smartphone-assisted RGB-dependent analytical sensing model is expected to show advantages in the on-site rapid detection of TC-PBQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of TC-PBQ in river water
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, we detected TC-PBQ in local river water. Within the detectable range of this analytical method, TC-PBQ was not detected. Meanwhile, TC-PBQ was not detected by by HPLC-MS analysis technology. Therefore, the corresponding spike recovery experiment was carried out. Spiked samples were prepared by adding TC-PBQ standard solutions (0.3, 2.0, 7.0 μM) to river water samples as detailed in Tables S1 and S2. The recovery of TC-PBQ detected by fluorescence spectrophotometer was 96.7%-103.3%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.3%-4.7%. The recovery of TC-PBQ detected by the analytical model means of RGB-dependent sensing assisted by a portable smartphone was 99.1%-106.7%, and the RSD was 2.8%-4.1%. HPLC-MS analysis technology was also adopted to validate the analysis method, and it can be seen that there was no significant difference in the analysis results. Then use the statistical tests to illustrate the accuracy of the experimental results [37]. Experimental results are compared at the usual 95% confidence level. It can be concluded that the analytical method is accurate because the calculated texp is less than t (0.025, 3) regardless of whether the true value is 100% (texp = 0.486 or 1.29, Equation 1, see SI) or the result of the HPLC-MS analytical technique (texp = 0.374 or 2.55, Equation 2, see SI). The results show that the dual-emission ratiometric CDs/MPA-capped CdTe sensing platform is effective in detecting TC-PBQ in real water samples. It also illustrates the reliability of the portable smartphone-assisted platform sensing analytical methods. On this basis, the ability of fluorescent test paper to detect TC-PBQ was studied. As shown in Fig. S4, after the river water sample spiked with TC-PBQ was dropped on the sensor-coated fluorescent test paper, the color of the test paper changed significantly and was similar to the change trend of the test paper when detecting the standard sample. Similarly, the RGB value of the color of the fluorescent test paper was obtained through the color recognition extractor in the portable smartphone for TC-PBQ analysis. The calculated recovery of TC-PBQ in river water samples ranged from 92.8% to 105.4%, which indicated that the analytical method for TC-PBQ by sensor-coated fluorescent test paper combined with a portable smartphone has the potential for rapid practical field application.

As mentioned in the introduction, little work has been done on the analytical detection of TC-PBQ, and most of the few existing studies do not provide information on analytical method data, including linear range, detection limit, and recovery of TC-PBQ. Therefore, there is no table for comparing TC-PBQ analytical methods. Rua et al. constructed a dual emission metal–organic framework detection TC-PBQ with a detection limit of 0.25 μM in the detection range of 0–100 μM [38]. Compared with this analytical methods, the TC-PBQ visual analytical methods developed in this experiment is not only more sensitive, but also the smart phone-assisted fluorescent test strip is easy to carry and can be used for on-site real-time detection.

In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed for the detection of TC-PBQ by adjusting the fluorescence intensity ratio of blue CDs and red MPA-capped CdTe as fluorescent materials. This dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor exhibited a wide range of color changes from peach red to pink, lilac purple, and finally bright blue when faced with TC-PBQ. Compared with organic fluorescent dyes, carbon quantum dots have the advantages of high stability and low toxicity, and the prepared sensor-coated fluorescent test paper are more portable than the probe solution, which is more convenient for rapid on-site visual detection. Through the analytical model of RGB-dependent sensing assisted by a portable smartphone, the RGB values of the probe solution and the fluorescent test paper in the presence of TC-PBQ were identified, which made the on-site rapid detection based on the fluorescent test paper more accurate. It can be found that there is a good linear relationship between the concentration of TC-PBQ and B/R, and the LOD of the probe solution and the fluorescent test paper are 80 nM and 93 nM, respectively. Finally, the good recovery (92.8%–105.4%) in spike recovery experiments of river water samples demonstrated the utility of this strategy for rapid on-site visual detection with TC-PBQ.

Meng Wu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Ting Liu: Validation, Investigation. Chenhui Yin: Methodology. Xinxin Jiang: Data curation. Qijun Sun: Software. Lei Gao: Funding acquisition. Na Niu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Ligang Chen: Funding acquisition. Huixin Gang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2022B004), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572022DJ01), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team), 111 Project (B20088), National Natural Science Foundation of China (32002445).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108686.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
